narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and an LSAT score of 33, your academic profile stood out in the law school admissions review process, which relies on a predictive model assessing the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 44.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam, a risk level high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of success. This prediction was shaped primarily by three key factors from your background: your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and Year 3 academic decile ranking of 3rd out of 10 in your school—well below the typical 5th or 6th decile for most students. Your undergraduate GPA emerged as the most influential positive factor, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, likely because your 2.90, while below the average of 3.21, still demonstrated a baseline of college-level achievement that the model views favorably in context. Similarly, your LSAT score of 33 worked in your favor as the second-most important influence, nudging the outlook toward bar passage despite being lower than the dataset average of 36.7, suggesting the model sees potential in your standardized test performance relative to similar profiles. However, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest counterproductive effect among these top factors, significantly increasing the predicted risk of failure by highlighting a dip in performance compared to peers, as this late-stage struggle stood out against the typical upward or stable trajectories in the data. Overall, while your full-time student status aligns with the vast majority of applicants and your upper-middle family income is solid, these top academic signals tipped the balance toward caution. You're not far off from stronger profiles—many admitted students have similar LSATs or GPAs but higher later deciles—and this rejection reflects a close call rather than a definitive flaw. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted coursework or tutoring, and applying to programs with more flexible bar passage thresholds. Gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience could also help reframe your story. With focused improvements, your profile has clear room to shine in the next admissions cycle. Keep pushing; many students in your position turn things around successfully.
